• States that W is the weight of the rider (and unicycle) and R is the push/reaction force (from the ground) • R and W are different types of force Or R and W act on the same object Or R and W are not equal. • They are not a N3 pair of forces MP3 conditional on MP2 (1) (1) (1)

• The resultant force acting in the vertical direction is zero so the unicycle will remain at that height Or The resultant force acting in the vertical direction is zero so zero acceleration in the vertical direction • The unicycle moves at a constant (forward) speed because the resultant horizontal force is zero or horizontal forces are balanced (because forward frictional force balances backward drag forces) (1) (1) Total for question 12 5 Number Answer Mark

• X is the force (or pull or tension) of the wire (on the flagpole) (accept tension in the wire) • Y is force (or reaction or push, ignore "normal") of the hinge (or wall) (on the flagpole). • Z is weight or force of gravity (of/on the flagpole) (1) (1) (1)

• Use of moment of a force = Fx • Use of the principle of moments • T = 323 (N) < 350 (N) so wire will not break Or Moment of weight about hinge = 88.3 (Nm) < 95.8 (Nm), max poss from wire Or Correct conclusion based on comparison of student's value with 350 N or 95.8 Nm MP3 depends on MP1 and MP2 being seen Example of calculation (15 kg × 9.81 N kg −1 × 0.6 m) − (T sin 20° × ⅔ × 1.2 m) = 0 T = (15 kg × 9.81 N kg −1 × 0.6 m) ÷ (0.8 m × sin 20°) = 88.29 Nm ÷ 0.2736 T = 322.7 N max available moment = 350 N × 0.8 m × sin 20° = 95.77 Nm > 88.29 Nm (1) (1) (1) Total for question 13 6 Number Answer Mark *14 This question assesses a student’s ability to show a coherent and logically structured answer with linkages and fully-sustained reasoning. Total marks awarded is the sum of marks for indicative content and the marks for structure and lines of reasoning Indicative content • Acceleration is maximum (or acceleration = g) initially • As velocity increases the air resistance increases • Resultant force decreases until it becomes zero and diver reaches terminal velocity Or (Eventually) forces balance and the diver reaches terminal velocity • At t the air resistance increases or is greater then the weight • Skydiver decelerates or the resultant force is now upwards/negative • Second terminal velocity is lower because air resistance = weight at a lower velocity. Ignore mention of upthrust. Number of marks awarded for structure of answer and sustained line of reasoning Answer shows a coherent and logical structure with linkages and full

(1) • Use of v = u + 2as (with u and v the correct way around) Or Loss of KE = gain of GPE (i.e. u V = 2gh) • See u sin 30° for initial vertical component of velocity u V • u = 57 (m s −1 ) Example of calculation = (u sin 30°) + (2× − 9.81 N kg −1 × 42 m) u V = u sin 30° = 28.7 m s −1 u = 57.4 m s −1 (1) (1) (1) • Use of trig to determine the horizontal component of the initial velocity METHOD 1 • Use of suitable equation(s) of motion to determine the time of flight • Use of v = s/t to determine the horizontal distance travelled by the flare • Comparison of distance to boat to distance flare travelled with conclusion consistent with student's value e.g. 7.9 km is less than 8.0 km so the flare can be seen. Example of calculation 0 = (57.4 m s − × sin 30° × t) − (0.5× 9.81 N kg − × t ) t = 5.85 s s = (57.4 m s −1 × cos 30° × 5.85 s) = 49.7 m s −1 × 5.85 s = 291 m (1) (1) (1) (1) × METHOD 2 • Use of v = s/t to determine the time to reach 200 m • Use of s = ut + ½at to find height reached after 200 m travel • Explains conclusion consistent with student's value e.g. flare above the sea and in range so visible
